coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric and selective fluorescent sensor for Fe(III) and bovine serum albumin based on energy transfer
coredata.dc:creator: He, Youling
Yu, Zhonghang
He, Jiangling
Zhang, Haoran
Liu, Yingliang
Lei, Bingfu
coredata.dc:description: A facility fluorescent sensor was fabricated for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ relying on red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) used as the target sensitive fluorophore. Fluorescence quenching are attributed to the strong coordination interaction between the target ions and the phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of RCDs. Interestingly, the fluorescent nanoprobe also showed great potential in detection of bovine serum albumin (BSA), attributed to the existence of Fe3+ ions. Serum albumins are the most abundant proteins spreaded throughout the circulatory system of all kinds of organisms, which play a crucial role in the process of metabolism. More importantly, the ratiometric fluorescent probe is easy to fabricate by mixturing blue emissive carbon dots (BCDs) with RCDs together, the fluorescence intensity of RCDs can be obviously quenched by Fe3+ ions or BSA under a single excitation wavelength, but that of BCDs is weakly changed, leading to the ratiometric fluorescence response with a good linear wider range of 0–400 μM and 0–320 μM, respectively. This ratiometric probe has also successfully been applied in real samples, that are cow urine and lake water, and observed to be almost no interference in the detection of Fe3+ ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Bovine serum albumin
Ratiometric sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) and blue emissive carbon dots (BCDs)
Preparation of the RCD-based and BCD-RCD-based ratiometric fluorescent probe
The morphology and structure of RCDs
Selectivity of RCD-based fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detections
Sensitivity of RCD-based fluorescent probe for Fe3+ and BSA detections
The BCD-RCD-based ratiometric sensor for selective and sensitive detections of Fe3+and BSA

Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51203053 and 21571067 ), the Project for Construction of a High-Level University in Guangdong Province , and the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. S2013030012842 ). The Guangzhou Science & Technology Program Project (Nos. 201605030005 , 201707010033 ). The Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (No. 2015B090903074 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrations of the fabrications of RCD-based fluorescent probe and the BCD-RCD-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for Fe3+ and BSA detections, respectively.
a) TEM and b) HRTEM images of RCDs. c) Wide-angle XRD pattern and d) FTIR spectrum of the RCDs.

a) UV–vis absorption (black line), normalized PL excitation (Ex) (λ635 = 500 nm, green line), and emission (Em) (λ500 = 635 nm, red line) spectra. b) PL emission spectra with different excitation wavelengths. c) and d) Comparison of fluorescence intensities of RCDs aqueous solution (10 mM) after the addition of different caions. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

a) Fluorescence quenching of the RCDs aqueous solution in the presence of Fe3+ ions (0–400 mM). b) Fluorescence quenching of the RCDs aqueous solution in the presence of BSA (0–800 mM). c) The curve of the fluorescence quenching values (I0-I)/I0 vs Fe3+ concentration ranging from 0 to 400 mM. The inset in c) is the linear calibration plot for Fe3+ detection. d) The curve of the fluorescence quenching values (I0-I)/I0 vs BSA concentration ranging from 0 to 800 mM. The inset in d) is the linear calibration plot for BSA detection.

a) Photographs of BCDs (left) and RCDs (right) aqueous solutions under UV light of 365 nm b) Comparison of fluorescence intensities of BCD/RCD mixture solution (10 mM) after the addition of different metal ions. Fluorescence quenching of the BCD/RCD mixture solution in the present of c) Fe3+ (0–400 mM) and e) BSA (0–800 mM), respectively. The curve of the fluorescence quenching values (R0-R)/R0 vs d) Fe3+ concentration ranging from 0 to 400 mM and f) BSA concentration ranging from 0 to 800 mM. The linear calibration plot for Fe3+ detection. The inset in f) is the linear calibration plot for BSA detection. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.

a) Normalized emission spectrum (ex: 340 nm) of the BCDs solution and excitation spectrum (em: 635 nm) of the RCDs solution. b) Emission spectra and c) CIE chromaticity diagram of the BCD/RCD mixtures were excited at 340 nm in 1.0 mg BCDs/1.0 mg, 2.0 mg, 3.0 mg, and 4.0 mg RCDs, respectively. d) Decay curves of BCDs at 417 nm emission in 1.0 mg BCDs/xRCDs (x: 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively) mixture at 340 nm excitation, respectively.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric and selective fluorescent sensor for Fe(III) and bovine serum albumin based on energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, 510642, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facility fluorescent sensor was fabricated for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ relying on red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) used as the target sensitive fluorophore. Fluorescence quenching are attributed to the strong coordination interaction between the target ions and the phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of RCDs. Interestingly, the fluorescent nanoprobe also showed great potential in detection of bovine serum albumin (BSA), attributed to the existence of Fe3+ ions. Serum albumins are the most abundant proteins spreaded throughout the circulatory system of all kinds of organisms, which play a crucial role in the process of metabolism. More importantly, the ratiometric fluorescent probe is easy to fabricate by mixturing blue emissive carbon dots (BCDs) with RCDs together, the fluorescence intensity of RCDs can be obviously quenched by Fe3+ ions or BSA under a single excitation wavelength, but that of BCDs is weakly changed, leading to the ratiometric fluorescence response with a good linear wider range of 0–400 μM and 0–320 μM, respectively. This ratiometric probe has also successfully been applied in real samples, that are cow urine and lake water, and observed to be almost no interference in the detection of Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Detections of ions and bioprotein concentrations are essential in industrial applications, chemical, biomedical, and as well as environmental sciences [1,2]. Specially, iron(III) is not only a kind of ubiquitous metal ion in living organisms, but also plays a pivotal role in various cell functions such as hemoglobin formation, brain function and muscle function [3]. Serum albumins are the most abundant proteins in the circulatory system of a wide variety of organisms, which are the majoy macromolecule contributing to the osmotic blood pressure [4,5]. Thus, the determination of Fe3+ or Serum albumins is of fundamental importance for the early identification and diagnosis of these diseases. Current detection assays including inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), atomic absorption spectrometry, voltammetry, and spectrophotometry, require tedious sample preparation procedures and sophisticated instrumentation, which hampers their pratical applications [6–8]. Recently, fluorescent probes are attracting considerable attention in highly sensitive and selective detections of metal ions, small molecules and explosives in the past decades owning to their spectral properties, such as multifunctionality, high surface-volume ratio, and ease of modification [9–11], which include of conjugated polymer, up-converting nanoparticles, organic molecules, and semiconductor quantum dots, but the relatively tedious synthesis, high phonon energies, non-renewable, toxicity and poor water solubility have limilted the detection of Fe3+ in biological system [12–14]. Therefore, the development of a novel fluorescent probe with low cytotoxicity, high water solubility, and excellent biocompatibity has become increasingly important and urgent.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescent probe are considered to replace conventional fluoroprobes because of their unique water solubility, tuable surface functionalities, nontoxic, small size and good photostability [15–17]. The most important of these being is direct interaction with the analytes, leading to the signal change of fluorescence intensity of CDs [18]. The surface groups is of great importance in researching CDs-based fluorescent probe, that of CDs are based on the raw materials. Such as the detection of iron (III), the quenching mechanism has been reported owning to the special coordination interaction between Fe3+ ions and the phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs [19]. On the basis of these unique properties, various CDs-based fluorescent probe for the detection of metal ions (Fe3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Hg+), bioproteins, and small organic molecules have been reported [20–22]. However, it is often challening in general to easy fabricate the CD-based fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of metal ions or bioprotein due to environmental interference, the low quantum yields (QYs) of CDs and nonspecificity [23]. Thus, the CDs-based ratiometric sensors have attracted more and more attention owning to their sensitivity and bult-in correction for environmental effect and other remarkable advantages [24]. The CDs-based ratiometric sensors is easy to simultaneously assemble diverse dyes, including of ions-sensitive and ions-insensitive on the same nanoparticle, to acquire ratiometric fluorescent sensors with tunable ions response range [25,26].

To order to achieve the aim, in the current study, we fabricated the red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) via a solvothermal method, p-phenylenediamine was used as the carbon source. The RCD-based fluorescent probe is with a highly selective and sensitive detection properties for Fe3+ with a linear range of 0–333.33 μM, which can be also applied in bovine serum albumin (BSA) test with a linear range of 0–200 μM, closely related to the existence of Fe3+. More importantly, the ratiometric fluorescent probe was fabricated by constructing RCDs and blue emissive carbon dots (BCDs) together with a higher sensitively in detection of Fe3+. The as-prepared BCDs can be facilely prepared by a hydrothermal process using polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as carbon precursors. The fluorescence intensity of RCDs can be obviously quenched by Fe3+ ions and BSA bioprotein, but that of BCDs is weakly changed, resulting in the ratiometric fluorescence response with a good linear wide range of 0–400 μM and 0–320 μM, a limit of detections are 0.45 μM and 0.56 μM, respectively.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with an ultraviolet-visible spectrofluorometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained from a XD-2X/M4600 X-ray diffract meter with Cu Ka radiation (40 kV, 20 mA, λ = 1.54051 Å). The data were collected in the 2θ range from 10° to 80° with scanning step of 0.02° and a scanning rate of 0.2° min−1. The particle morphology was analysed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, TECNAI12, Holland). The infrared spectrum was recorded on a Fourier spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere using KBr as a reference. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained with a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a monochromator (resolution: 0.2 nm) and a 150-W Xe lamp as the excitation source. The decay lifetimes were measured on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (HORIBA, JOBIN YVON FL3-21). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed by using a VG Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (VG Scientific) operated at 120 W. AFM images were recorded with Bruker multimode 8. TGA data were recorded by DTG-60 (Japan Shimadzu).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) and blue emissive carbon dots (BCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: RCDs and BCDs were synthesized based on the reported work, according to the literature and, respectively [27,28]. Detailedly, the mixture of p-phenylenediamines (0.5 g) and 50 mL of ethanol was heated at 160 °C for 14 h through solvothermal method. Then obtained reaction product was cooled to room temperature naturally, the crude product was removed by centrifugation (12000 r min−1, 15 min), and the dark-red suspension was collected. The suspension was then purified with silica column chromatography using mixtures of methylene chloride and methanol as eluents. To obtain more uniformly sized CDs, the as-prepared CDs solution was further dialyzed in water using a membrane for 3 days. After removing the solvents and further drying under freeze-drying treatment, the dark-red powder was obtained.

Meanwhile, the BCDs were obtained. Detailedly, PVA (1 g) and ethylenediamine (EDA, 0.75 mL) were first dissolved in deionized water (20 mL). The mixture was transferred to Teflon autoclave (volume of mL) and heated at 220 °C for 10 h. After this reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The brown suspension was obtained. Then an enormous amount of acetone was added to the suspension to precipitate the excess PVA. The precipitation was separated by centrifugation (12000 rpm). Finally, the transparent and brown solution was purified by dialysis (the molecular weight cut off is 1000 Da) in deionized water. The yellow CDs powder was then obtained by freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the RCD-based and BCD-RCD-based ratiometric fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) can be facilely obtained via a solvothermal method of p-phenylenediamine and ethanol at 160 °C for 14 h on the basis of the previous report (Supporting Information) [27]. In this method, p-phenylenediamine was used as the carbon source. As seen from Scheme 1 , the fluorescence of RCDs solution was serious quenched with the gradually addition of Fe3+. In the bioprotein application, the quenching effect of the RCDs after addition of bovine serum albumin (BSA) can be also observed. More importantly, the ratiometric sensing can be fabricated by constructing RCDs and blue-emissive carbon dots (BCDs) together, which also detected Fe3+ and BSA, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology and structure of RCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the RCDs indicate uniform nanoparticles with average sizes distribution from 6 to 8 nm (Fig. 1 a) and crystalline structure from the obvious lattice fringes (Fig. 1b). The lattice fringe space is calculated to be about 0.34 nm, greatly agree with the (002) planes of the graphite, displaying that the crystalline core of RCD is with graphite structure. Moreover, the peak at 22○ (0.34 nm) in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 1c) was assigned to highly disordered carbon atoms [29]. In the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum analysis of RCDs, the following were observed: stretching vibrations of CH at 2840–2910 cm−1, CN = at 1330–1380 cm−1, and CO at 1230–1260 cm−1 (Fig. 1d) [30,31]. It implies decomposition, intermolecular cyclization, and condensation reactions occurred during the forming of RCDs.

In ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum of the RCDs, a higher-energy absorption band was observed λ = 280 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transitions of the conjugated CC units from the carbon core, leading to weak observed fluorescence emission (Fig. 2 a) [32]. In the lower-energy region, abroad absorption band centered at 500 nm were observed, which was assigned to the n-π* transition from the surface/molecular section, resulting in strong fluorescence emission [33]. In the fluorescence spectra, the RCDs have optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 500 nm and 635 nm, and show no excitation dependence (Fig. 2b). As expected, the relatively highly quantum yields (QYs) reach up to 28% (Fig. S1) [34]. The fluorescence was demonstrated with a high optical stability (Fig. S2). The phenolic hydroxyl group may be an indispensable part for the RCDs, that of RCDs can be applied in multifunctional ions detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of RCD-based fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detections
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the RCDs to various cations (Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Li+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Hg+, NH4+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Na+, Ni+, Ag+, Zn2+, Fe3+) at the concentration of 50 μM, significant fluorescence quenching effects were observed with addition of Fe3+, while other anion ions showed only a weak quenching effect and the influence was almost negligible (Fig. 2c–d) [35]. To infer the mechanism based on the recent research, the quenched phenomenon of the RCD-Fe3+ was contributed to complex reaction between the RCDs and Fe3+, which accelerated the non-radiative electron in the excited state to the d orbital of Fe3+ through an effective electron transfer process, leading to a substantial decrease of the fluorescence of RCDs. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching of CDs-based complex can effectively recovered due to the breaking of quenching and recombination of the quenchers with the targeted analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of RCD-based fluorescent probe for Fe3+ and BSA detections
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching of the RCDs was shown with the addition of Fe3+ from 0 to 400 μM (Fig. 3 a). The fluorescence signal (I0-I)/I0 of RCDs gradually faded as the increasing concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 400 μM, experimental data tend to a down a curved nonlinear curve, it carried out the environmental and operational factors (Fig. 3c), the plots may be well fitted by using a Demas two-site mode (In the Supporting Information) [36]. The value of (I0-I)/I0 shows a good linear coefficient square (R2) of 0.9946 from 0 to 333.33 μM and the a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.48 μM (the inset of Fig. 3c) was calculated from 3σ/k, where 3 is referred to signal to noise ratio, based on the ratio of signal-to-noise of 3, σ is the standard deviation of blank measurement of 10 times and k is the slope of the calibration curve. The surface atomic concentration of iron element of bovine serum albumin (BSA) is 0.089%, estimated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) in Fig. S3 and atomic emission spectrometer (ICP) element analysis in Table S1. The mechanism of BSA quenching could also be the strong coordination interaction between a small amount of Fe3+ and the phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of RCDs. In application of biological protein, their PL intensity gradually quenched with the increasing of BSA from 0 to 800 μM (Fig. 3b), the value of (I0-I)/I0 shows a good linear correlation square (R2) of 0.9912 over the wide range from 0 to 200 μM (the inset of Fig. 3d), the LOD was estimated to be 0.66 μM [37]. The linear relationship is applied to the Stern-Volmer (SV) equation (In Supporting Information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The BCD-RCD-based ratiometric sensor for selective and sensitive detections of Fe3+and BSA
ce:section.ce:para: The previously reported CDs mostly show strong emission only in the blue-light region. Furthermore, the emission spectra of as-prepared RCDs have no excitation dependence with a peak of 635 nm (Fig. 2b). Naturally, the ratiometric sensors were also developed, which is based on the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two wavelengths, is thus less interfered and more reliable [38]. The as-prepared BCDs can be facilely prepared by a hydrothermal process using polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) according to a previous report. The basic fluorescent properties and morphologies can be seen from Fig. S4, BCDs reveal uniform nanoparticles with average sizes distribution from 5 to 6 nm. The BCDs and RCDs aqueous solutions monotonously show blue and red lights under UV light of 365 nm (Fig. 4 a). The relevant characteristics were carried out to successfully obtain BCDs, indicating uniform nanoparticles with average sizes distribution from 6 to 8 nm and crystalline structure and strongest emission peak at 417 nm under 340-nm excitation (Fig. S4). The QYs reach 54% (Fig. S5). Furthermore, almost no shift in the peak of the absorption spectra of BCDs upon successive addition of RCDs confirmed that BCDs did not undergo aggregation upon addition of RCDs (Fig. S6) [39].

The BCD/RCD mixture solution exhibits two characteristic peaks under a single wavelength. To demonstrate the ratiometric fluorescence probe can be successfully fabricated, first, the selectivity of the BCD/RCD mixture solution was demonstrated in the present different metal ions (Fig. 4b). Then, the fluorescence quenching of the BCD/RCD mixture was only presented with addition of Fe3+ (Fig. 4c). The PL intensity (peak at 635 nm) of the mixture decreased obviously and that of the mixture (peak at 417 nm) weakly decreased from 0 to 400 μM. The ratio of the RCD (635 nm) and BCD (417) peaks as a function of Fe3+ were potted for BCD/RCD. The (R0-R)/R0 plots are fitted by [Eq. (7)] (In the Supporting Information). A good linear shows a wider range from 0 to 400 μM and the LOD is 0.45 μM (Fig. 4d). Simultaneously, this RCD-based and ratiometric BCD-RCD-based fluorescence probes have been also applied in the detection of Fe3+ in the lake water and urine of the cattle (Fig. S7). Likewise, the ratiometric (BCDs/RCDs) sensor has also applied in BSA detection from 0 to 800 μM (Fig. 4e), A good linear reveals a wider range from 0 to 320 μM and a limit of detection is 0.56 μM, (Inset of Fig. 4f). Comparatively speaking, it is clearly to observed, the two kinds of ratiometric probes exhibit wider detection range and more sensitive detection limit than the single probe, the ratiometric (BCD/RCD) sensing system has highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ or BSA.

Further investigating sensing mechanism of the BCD/RCD mixture, energy transfer process between BCDs and RCDs can be shown. Firstly, the emission spectrum of BCDs and the excitation spectrum of the RCDs have significantly overlaps (Fig. 5 a). Then, the characteristic peak intensity of the BCDs at 417 nm monotonously weakens, and that of the RCDs at 635 nm enhances gradually with the increasing of the RCDs (Fig. 5b). Naturally, the emission colors of the BCD/RCD mixture are easily controlled through adjusting the relative mass of BCD/RCD from blue to white-region, and then from white to red-light region (Fig. 5c and Table S2). Furthermore, these decay lifetimes of the BCDs become shorter, and the energy transfer efficiency (φ) and the probability (P) gradually increase with increasing RCD content (Fig. 5d, Table S3). The evidences confirm the energy transfer between BCD donor and RCD acceptor [40,41]. The relevant equations have shown in the Supporting Information.

In summary, we synthesized the red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) via a solvothermal method with a high fluorescence stability. The chemical structure and PL mechanism were demonstrated in detail. Moreover, the RCDs reveal a potential application as a sensing platform with a highly selective and sensitive detection properties for Fe3+ with a linear range of 0–333.33 μM, attributed to forming of the quenchers between the phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of RCDs and the target ions. Furthermore, the RCDs could be utilized as a biosensor reagent capable detection of BSA with a linear range of 0–200 μM, attributed to the existence of Fe3+. More importantly, the ratiometric fluorescent probe is easy to fabricate by mixturing blue emissive carbo dots (BCDs) with RCDs together, and has a highly more sensitive in the detection of Fe3+ evenly in real samples of lake water and cow urine. A good linear with the wide range from 0 to 400 μM was obtained for Fe3+ detection, and a limit of detection (LOD) is 0.45 μM. A good linear with the wide range from 0 to 320 μM was obtained for BSA, the LOD is 0.56 μM. It is believed that the as-prepared RCD may offer a higher sensitive and selective sensor in environmental applications and biological sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51203053 and 21571067), the Project for Construction of a High-Level University in Guangdong Province, and the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. S2013030012842). The Guangzhou Science & Technology Program Project (Nos. 201605030005, 201707010033). The Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (No. 2015B090903074).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.01.242.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Youling He received her Bachelor majoring in Pharmaceutical Engineering from Wuhan Institute of Bioengineering, Wuhan, China in 2015, and then she began her Master of Chemical Engineering student experience under the supervising of Prof. Bingfu Lei since 2014 in South China Agricultural University (SCAU). Her current research focus on the assembled materials of fluorescent carbon dots doped mesoporous aluminas.

Zhonghang Yu will receive his Bachelor majoring in Material Engineering from South China Agricultural University (SCAU), Guangzhou, China in 2018. His current research focuses on the research of the assembled compound of rare earth assembled mesoporous materials.

Jiangling He received her Bachelor majoring in Chemistry from Hubei Engineering University, Xiaogan, China in 2014, and then she began her Master of Chemical Engineering student experience under the supervising of Prof. Yingliang Liu since 2014 in South China Agricultural University (SCAU). Her research focused on the research of preparation and properties of carbon dots.

Haoran Zhang received her MSc in 2004 in Inorganic Chemistry from Northeast Normal University (NNU) in China, and then she received her PhD in 2011 in Jinan University (JNU). Then she has been working as a vice-professor at SCAU. Her current research is synthesis and application of organic-inorganic hybrid compound used for sensing and display.

Yingliang Liu received his MSc degree in Chemistry in 1989 from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry of Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), and PhD in 1994 in Inorganic Chemistry from Sun Yat-Sen University. He has been a professor at the JUN since 2002 and moved from JNU to SCAU at 2012. He has been working on the topics including luminescence materials, functional glass and photoelectron materials.

Bingfu Lei received his BSc in 2001 in Food and Science Engineering and MSc in 2004 in Inorganic Chemistry from Jinan University, and PhD in 2007 in Condensed Matter Physics from Changchun Institute of Optics Fine Mechanics and Physics of Chinese Academy of Sciences, and then he became a JSPS Postdoctoral Fellowship for Foreign Researchers at the Center for Advanced Science and Innovation of Osaka University from 2007 to 2009. He obtained a professor position at SCAU since 2012. His current research interests mainly focus on the exploration of inorganic phosphors, organic-inorganic hybrid materials, and covalently grafted hybrid materials for gas sensors, chemosensors and high luminescent display devices.
